expectations.text: The response explains the memory snapshot (sleep/wake) approach for cold-start reduction
expectations.evidence: The solution does not mention memory snapshots or a sleep/wake container lifecycle approach at all. It covers six strategies: baking weights into the image layer, keep_warm, container_idle_timeout, CUDA warmup in @modal.enter(), quantization, and tensor parallelism avoidance. None of these is the snapshot/restore pattern.

expectations.text: The response explains the tradeoffs of using memory snapshots (complexity, limitations)
expectations.evidence: Memory snapshots are never introduced in the response, so their tradeoffs are not discussed. This expectation cannot pass given the absence of the topic.

expectations.text: The response mentions keeping containers warm via min_containers or similar knobs
expectations.evidence: Option 2 in solution.md is dedicated to keep_warm: 'keep_warm=1  # always keep 1 container hot'. It includes code examples, tradeoff analysis (zero cold-start for warm replicas vs. idle GPU cost at ~$3-4/hr), and guidance on tuning container_idle_timeout alongside keep_warm.

expectations.text: The response addresses Volume-cached weights as a prerequisite baseline
expectations.evidence: The response uses image.run_function() to bake weights into the container image layer, not Modal Volumes. modal.Volume is not mentioned anywhere in the solution. These are distinct mechanisms: Volume-cached weights persist across container restarts without rebuilding the image; the image-baking approach requires an image rebuild to update weights. The expectation's specific prerequisite (Volume-cached weights) is not addressed.

expectations.text: The response provides enough signal to choose between approaches based on the use case
expectations.evidence: The 'Recommended Strategy for Bursty Traffic' section ranks all options in priority order with rationale. The summary table compares all six options across Cold-Start Impact, Cost Impact, and Complexity dimensions. The tradeoff sections under each option (Pro/Con lists) further support decision-making.

expectations.text: The response acknowledges that snapshot-based approaches add operational complexity and are only warranted when cold-start latency is a first-class requirement
expectations.evidence: Snapshot-based approaches are not discussed anywhere in the response, so this contextual acknowledgment is absent.

claims.claim: Cold starts of 3+ minutes typically stem from three main bottlenecks: image pull, weight download, and vLLM engine init
claims.evidence: This is an accurate characterization of the typical cold-start breakdown for GPU containers running vLLM.

claims.claim: A 7B model in bfloat16 is ~14 GB; AWQ 4-bit reduces it to ~4 GB
claims.evidence: 7B parameters * 2 bytes (bfloat16) = ~14 GB is correct. AWQ 4-bit roughly halves twice (~3.5 GB), so ~4 GB is a reasonable approximation.

claims.claim: With weights baked in and one warm container, initialization time drops from 3+ minutes to 30-60 seconds
claims.evidence: This is stated as an approximation based on typical A100 benchmarks (noted in the transcript). Without empirical measurement it is unverifiable, but is a plausible estimate for vLLM engine init time excluding the download phase.

claims.claim: A100 GPU costs approximately $3-4/hr on Modal
claims.evidence: Modal pricing is not verifiable from the available outputs and changes over time. The figure is in the right ballpark for A100 compute but should be treated as approximate.

claims.claim: hf_transfer (Rust-based parallel downloader) can reduce download phase from minutes to tens of seconds
claims.evidence: Plausible claim, but no benchmark data is cited. hf_transfer is a real tool that does improve download speed, but exact speedup depends on network conditions and the worker environment.

suggestions.assertion: The response explains the memory snapshot (sleep/wake) approach for cold-start reduction
suggestions.reason: This expectation presupposes a specific technique (memory snapshots / sleep-wake lifecycle) that is a non-obvious, advanced Modal feature. Without_skill runs should not be expected to know about it unless it is widely documented. The eval is effectively testing whether the model happens to know a niche technique, which means a well-grounded general answer on cold-start reduction will always fail this expectation even if the advice is excellent. Consider whether this expectation belongs in a 'without_skill' baseline eval at all, or whether it should only apply to the 'with_skill' condition.

suggestions.assertion: The response addresses Volume-cached weights as a prerequisite baseline
suggestions.reason: The solution correctly recommends image-layer weight baking as an alternative to Volume-cached weights, and for many use cases image baking is actually simpler. The framing of Volume caching as a 'prerequisite baseline' rather than one option among several may be too prescriptive. An assertion that checks 'the response distinguishes between weight-baking strategies and explains their tradeoffs' would be more discriminating and would capture both approaches.

No expectation checks whether the response correctly identifies that keep_warm addresses only the warm replica — additional replicas during traffic spikes still cold-start. The solution does cover this ('Does not help when traffic spikes beyond the warm pool size'), but an explicit assertion would catch responses that oversell keep_warm as a complete solution.

eval_feedback.overall: Three of the four failing expectations hinge on a single absent topic (memory snapshots), which is a niche technique unlikely to be known without specific skill guidance. The eval as written sets a high bar for the without_skill baseline and may not fairly distinguish between a genuinely poor response and a good general response that lacks knowledge of a Modal-specific advanced feature. The keep_warm and signal-quality expectations are well-calibrated.
